text: After administration of general anesthesia, the patient is placed in a full lateral decubitus position with the table flexed maximally.
This creates optimal room between inner spaces as well as preventing the hip from colliding with the camera arm.
A forearm approach is employed with the camera arm in the atherosclerosis space in the posterior axillary line.
For left-sided procedures, two additional arms are placed posterior to the tip of the scapula, and the fourth arm is placed in a mid-axillary incision.
All incisions are 10 to 12 millimeters with the exception of the axillary incision, which is a three centimeter non-rib spreading utility incision.
Once the patient is prepped and draped, local anesthesia is used to create subcutaneous and intercostal blocks.
The procedure is initiated by first making the camera port incision.
Because this incision is placed in the anterior costrophrenic sulcus, it is preferable to enter the chest bluntly to avoid injury to the diaphragm or intra-abdominal contents.
The eight millimeter robotic scope is placed in the chest to do an initial exploration and rule out intrapleural disease, as well as to help guide placement of the other ports.
Once each of the port access incisions have been made, the arms are deployed for docking and side docked using the laser sighting to position the arms relative to the camera port.
The camera arm is attached to the camera port
Use of the patient clearance button before docking the other arms is very useful to avoid collisions.
The camera is placed into the chest and the intraoperative laser sighting is used to target the anatomy of interest.
This allows for rotation of the arms into ideal position and the arms are then attached to each of the ports.
Each individual patient clearance button is used to provide optimal spacing between the arms to once again avoid external collisions.
Each instrument is introduced under direct vision with the thoracoscope until all three instruments are positioned.
Traditional segmentectomy begins with isolation of the pulmonary artery.
In this view, the anterior fissure of the left lower lobe has already been divided.
One can identify the tumor in the anterior segment of the left lower lobe.
Initial dissection is aimed at identifying the bronchus and the artery and its relation.
The pleura over the pulmonary artery is carefully dissected distally, exposing the branches of the basilar pulmonary artery in order to isolate the specific anterior branch.
Effective retraction of the surrounding tissue by the non-dominant hand is critical in providing the exposure necessary to gently dissect and isolate the hyalur structures.
A combination of blunt and precise electrocautery dissection can be used to isolate structures safely.
In this case, the anterior branch of the basal pulmonary artery is being isolated using a blunt tip forcep.
Once the hilar structure of interest is isolated, the ability to open the robotic instrument completely behind it ensures that it may be ligated effectively.
In this case, the anterior branch of the pulmonary artery will be divided through the use of a non-robotic stapler passed through the anterior axillary incision.
This can be done by the first assistant and requires that the arm is temporarily moved out of the incision.
Use of the curved tip stapling devices, either traditional or robotic, can greatly facilitate passage of the stapler behind the vessel.
Regardless of the method of ligation, it is imperative that it is performed with no visual evidence of excessive tension or torque, particularly in the case of the pulmonary vessels.
Following dissection and ligation of the pulmonary artery,
The segmental bronchus is next dissected.
One can see here that the pulmonary artery stump leads directly to the bronchus.
Dissection must be performed distally in order to isolate the specific bronchus going to the segment of interest.
In the case of non-palpable lesions, it may be necessary to perform intraoperative
localization of the lesion in order to ensure that the correct segment is being removed.
Once the distal branches have been exposed, again, a curved tip endovascular stapler may be passed to divide the segmental bronchus of the lung.
The last remaining hilar structure is the pulmonary vein.
In most cases, the pulmonary veins run with the intersegmental planes and can be divided with the intersegmental planes.
In the case of the anterior basilar segment, the vein is easily isolated from the inferior vein at the anterior portion of the hilum of the lung.
The vein typically is more robust than the pulmonary artery.
And if the need arises to grab it with the vascular forceps, this is acceptable.
Once it is isolated, it may be similarly stapled with the endovascular stapler.
In this case, the stapler is passed from the posterior incision as this strategy provides a more direct angle of approach to divide the vessel.
Identification of the segmental fissures can be challenging, particularly with a single basilar segmentectomy.
Once the isocyanine green is reconstituted in saline, it can be injected through a peripheral IV.
Within 10 to 15 seconds there is excellent circulation through the pulmonary system.
Use of intraoperative fluorescence imaging shows the well demarcated lung of the segment that is to be removed.
Electrocautery is used to mark the segmental fissures under direct vision.
In this instance one can see
the isocyanine green coursing through the intact pulmonary vasculature.
Once identified, the segmental fissures can then be divided, either using electrocautery, fracture technique, or stapling.
We prefer the use of endovascular staplers in order to limit bleeding and the potential for postoperative air leak.
A combination of large and medium to large staple loads of varying lengths between 30, 45, and 60 millimeters may be employed to divide the fissures.
It is often most feasible to remove the majority of the robotic instruments in order to provide adequate exposure to manipulate the lung and identify the fissures.
Once the specimen is completely separated, we recommend removal from the chest using a bag of some sort.
We prefer a laparotomy sac because of its durability and typically employ the mid-axillary incision to remove the specimen.
Following removal of the specimen, multilevel intercostal blocks with 0.5% marking are created and a single 28 French chest tube is placed through the camera port incision.
